EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms over the San Bernardino Mountains caused flooding of homes, businesses, and roads around Big Bear City and mud slides just west of the Cajon Pass. As the runoff came down the north slopes, flood waters two feet deep covered Highway 247 near Pony Road.
A female hiker and a male softball umpire suffered heat exhaustion as the temperature rose to 115 degrees over the Coachella Valley.

Lightning struck a cable line connected to a house, which then jumped to the metal roof flashing and started a fire along the roof eaves on the opposite side of the house.  The resident was able to quickly douse the flames with a garden hose.

A 14 year old girl was swept out 30 yards by a rip current. A 16 year old boy was able to bring her back to shore, but she did not have a pulse nor was she breathing. Lifeguards were able to resuscitate her, then sent both teens to the hospital for further treatment.
Eight acres of dry brush were consumed before the fire was brought under control.
A tractor, picking up bales of hay, started a brush fire that consumed 5 acres of dry brush and 70 bales of hay before fire fighters brought the fire under control.
A 283 acre brush fire occurred in the Good Hope area of Riverside County.
A brush fire burnt 350 acres near East Benton and Balinger roads in rough hilly terrain.

A weed cutter caused a spark that grew into a 548 acre brush fire between Poppet Flats and Twin Pines. The blaze, fueled by 10 to 15 mph winds, jumped Highway 243, forcing the closure of that road. The community of Twin Pines was evacuated. One house was damaged. Cost of fighting the blaze was estimated at $400,000.
A car was struck by lightning.
Wind gust to 40 mph in downdrafts lowered the visibility to near zero from blowing sand. Heavy rain, 0.75 inches in 20 minutes, was also observed.
A 20 acre brush fire consumed a 100 year old farmhouse and a garage. Firefighters were able to save two other homes in the path of the flames.
A four acre brush fire alongside Interstate 5 briefly threatened some homes in Carlsbad.

A spark from a passing train started a brush fire that grew to 685 acres. The blaze briefly threatened the community of Lytle Creek, power transmission lines, and a ranch where animals are trained for movies. No injuries occurred, nor were any structures burned.

A thunderstorm dropped over an inch of rain on Sugar Loaf at the 7000 foot elevation and then moved down the north slope. The ground was already saturated from earlier rains during the day. The combined effect produced another flash flood on the desert floor across Highway 247 near Pony Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time approximate - based on relayed spotter report and radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spotter report of 3/4 inch hail, very heavy rain, and some street flooding with water two inches deep in some areas.
Mono County Sheriffs Office reported that very heavy rain caused a rock and mud slide that closed Highway 120 near Tioga Pass for approximately 2 1/2 hours.  No injuries or damage were reported.
Spotter report of 0.88 inch hail.
Spotter report of 0.75 inch hail.
Spotter report of 0.75 inch hail 5 miles east of Truckee.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced 1.54 inches of rain in a two hour period which caused several roads to flood.
Heavy rain caused severe flooding of area roadways in Joshua Tree. Flooding was reported to be over one foot deep in many areas.
Heavy rains from thunderstorms caused significant flooding of area roadways near Victorville. Water was reported to be two feet deep in places.

A tornado touched down in Joshua Tree causing minor damage to homes and businesses. The most significant damage reports included damage to a roof of an abandoned house and a patio cover torn off another home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Frost Warning was issued for the above zones at 1544 PDT on 07/31/01.  Observed low temperatures ranged from 40 to 48, which was likely due to overnight winds in the area.  The winds and the warmer than expected temperatures made frost very unlikely this night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: See below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Flash Flood Warning was issued for Southwest Siskiyou county for the period listed above, based on radar data.  Verification of the warning was attempted, but was unsuccessful.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: See below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Flash Flood Warning was issued for Central Siskiyou county for the period listed above, emphasizing the Yreka area, based on radar data.  Several spotters reported heavy rain and one reported that Mill Creek was at bankfull.  However, no report verifying a flash flood was received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: See below.
Winds blew down 16 power poles in Blythe and caused severe damage to a mobile home.
Secondary roads closed due to very heavy rainfall.  One report indicated  1.90 inches in about 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over 100 surf rescues of people caught in rip currents were performed by lifeguards. The combination of the weekend, south swells with breakers of 5 feet, and inland temperatures over 100 degrees had lured many people to the beach.

A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued for North Central Siskiyou county in California and Southwest Jackson county in Oregon for the period listed above, based on radar data.  Verification of the warning was attempted but was unsuccessful.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: See below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gust front from thunderstorms over Southeast California moved northwest across the Imperial Valley and Salton Sea. The 30 mph  winds caused a dust storm with visibility less than 1 mile over eastern San Diego County, the Coachella Valley, and in the Banning Pass.

A gust front from thunderstorms over Southeast California moved northwest across the Imperial Valley and Salton Sea. The 30 mph  winds caused a dust storm with visibility less than 1 mile over eastern San Diego County, the Coachella Valley, and in the Banning Pass.
Dense fog for the second night in a row contributed to several traffic accidents.

Sub-tropical moisture streamed over portions of Interior Central California during the 3rd.  This prompted a Flash Flood Watch due to expected locally heavy rain in the Kern County Deserts and Mountains.  Only small amounts of rain fell on the San Joaquin Valley floor but this did cause concern from agriculture during the crop harvest season.  No damage was reported.
Dense fog for the third night in a row caused widespread traffic problems during the morning commute in San Diego County.
Dense fog for the third night in a row caused widespread traffic problems during the morning commute in San Diego County.
A rogue 10 foot wave capsized an 18 foot fishing boat off of BIrd Rock in La Jolla and injured the two occupants.

Sub-tropical moisture streamed over portions of Interior Central California during the 3rd.  This prompted a Flash Flood Watch due to expected locally heavy rain in the Kern County Deserts and Mountains.  Only small amounts of rain fell on the San Joaquin Valley floor but this did cause concern from agriculture during the crop harvest season.  No damage was reported.

Sub-tropical moisture streamed over portions of Interior Central California during the 3rd.  This prompted a Flash Flood Watch due to expected locally heavy rain in the Kern County Deserts and Mountains.  Only small amounts of rain fell on the San Joaquin Valley floor but this did cause concern from agriculture during the crop harvest season.  No damage was reported.
A large dust devil, 400 feet high and 100 feet wide, destroyed an outdoor sign, toppled a portable toilet, and rolled a telephone pole log lying on the ground 20 feet.
A brush fire consumed 3 acres in Mission Valley just east of Interstate 5.

A brush fire quickly grew to 700 acres due to downdraft winds from a thunderstorm, dry brush, and hot temperatures. The flames came within several hundred yards of ranch structures. Three of the 500 firefighters assigned to the fire were overcome by heat exhaustion and injuries.

A wild fire in a steep canyon near the main toll plaza on the San Joaquin Hills Toll Road, Highway 73, was quickly contained by 75 firefighters, 3 helicopters, 2 airtankers, and the presence of high humidity and lack of strong canyon winds.

A gust front from thunderstorms over Southeast California moved northwest across the Imperial Valley and Salton Sea. The 30 mph  winds caused a dust storm with visibility less than 1 mile over eastern San Diego County, the Coachella Valley, and in the Banning Pass.

Mono County Sheriff reported heavy rain that caused several mudslides.  A mudslide on Highway 395 five miles north of Walker restricted traffic to one lane.  Two separate slides on Highway 120 trapped 3 cars and closed Tioga Pass.  No injuries or damage were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind gusts blew down several power poles.

Strong thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall caused washes and secondary roads to be flooded quickly.  A camp ground near Senator Wash Reservoir flooded and campers moved to higher ground to escape the flood waters.  State Highway 78, between Glamis and and S34 Ogilby Road, was closed due to the flood waters.

A large thunderstorm complex which had developed a gust front up to 60 miles long in south central Arizona moved to the southwest into Yuma County Arizona and eastern Imperial County, California.  As the gust front moved west across the Colorado River into Winterhaven, about 30 large trees were uprooted.
Severe thunderstorm wind gusts uprooted trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Hanford National Weather Service Office relayed a report of heavy rain that produced a mud and rock slide that closed Highway 120 below Tioga Pass.  Some hail was observed near 11,000 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: See the Hoover Complex Fire in the August 2001 Stormdata for details.
The Burnt Fire details are available in the August 2001 Stormdata.

The Tar Fire was ignited by lightning on September 4th near Atwell Grove.  The fire burned 505 acres and was managed as a resource benefit throughout the remainder of the month of September.  There were no fatalities, injuries, or structures lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Part of the remnants of Hurricane Flossie, moved across Southwest California over a three day period.On 09/01/01, thunderstorms formed over the mountains and deserts of San Diego and Riverside Counties. Many reported small hail ranging in diameter from a quarter inch to a half inch. On 9/2/01, thunderstorms formed over all the mountains. Rainfall of over 2 inches in one hour was observed. Flooding and mudslides across major roadways occurred between Barton Flats and Forest Falls, from Oak Glen south to Cherry Valley  and into the Banning Pass near Beaumont, and around Lake Cuyamaca. Frequent lightning was recorded, with lightning detection sensors indicating about 60 strikes per hour. Numerous small fires were started in the National Forests. A group of four hikers climbing Stonewall Peak in the San Diego County Mountains encountered lightning and heavy rain. Two of the hikers stopped short of the peak and took shelter. Two others continued on to the lookout area which is surrounded by a metal railing. The 53 year old man was struck in the head by lightning and died. The other, a 13 year old boy, was knocked unconscious but survived. Small hail was observed by many. A downdraft from a thunderstorm knocked a tree onto a house in Beaumont. On 9/3/01, afternoon thunderstorms formed over the San Bernardino Mountains and the Apple Valley. Two boys in an open field in Apple Valley were hit by lightning. The 13 year old was eating from a cup of noodles when lightning hit him in the head and exited his groin area. He was pronounced dead some time later after CPR efforts were unsuccessful. The 9 year old boy standing next to him was holding a metal tool  at the time. He "felt the lightning pick (him) up", and then awoke lying on the ground unable to feel his legs. He suffered burns to his right waist, left calf, left foot, and dehydration. Heavy downpours from the same thunderstorm caused street flooding as it moved further east.

A brush fire burned 30 acres before it was brought under control. Cool temperatures and lack of wind aided firefighters.
A two acre brush fire caused minor damage to vacant buildings near the San Bernardino International Airport.
A 49 year old male surfer nearly drowned in the surf off Crystal Cove. He was rescued by a lifeguard and a bystander.

A wildfire burned 60 acres, destroyed several storage sheds, scorched several residences, and forced the evacuation of residents and livestock before it was brought under control by more than 200 firefighters.
A 19year old man disappeared in the surf. Lifeguards recovered his body after a 90 minute search.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hoover Complex Fire started by lightning on July 13, 2001, burned through August, and was still burning toward the end of September.  The first spot forecast was received on August 8th and the fire burned about 8040 acres.  The fire was shifted as a resource benefit during late August.  There were no fatalities, injuries, or structures lost.  Cost to contain estimated at $777.8K by 9/21/01.
See the Hoover Complex Fire in the August 2001 Stormdata for details.

Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down several power lines near City Hall and the local library and both  buildings had to be evacuated. One of the power lines also fell onto a womans car trapping her for over half an hour. The woman was uninjured, but the car received minor damage.
Details on the Burnt Fire can be found in the August 2001 Stormdata.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Frost Warning was issued for the above zones this night. Reported low temperatures ranged from 31 to 40, with most stations reporting low temperatures in the 35 to 40 range. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Highway Fire is suspected to have been caused by arson.  It burned 4152 acres and forced the evacuation of a large number of residents.  There were no fatalities but 8 structures were lost (3 primary and 5 out-buildings).  Total cost to contain was over $6M.

Gusty wind across the San Luis Reservoir in Western Merced County reached sustained Lake Wind Advisory criteria by the late morning on the 29th.  Some wind at 27 gusting to 32 MPH occurred at 0725 PST ... but sustained wind of 33 MPH with gusts over 40 MPH occurred during the afternoon hours.

Traffic on Interstate 15 was delayed for 25 minutes while firefighters fought a one acre fire in a steep patch of brush and pepper trees.
A ten acre brush fire along Interstate 215 and Watkins drive burned to the freeway shoulder and caused traffic to back up.
The body of an 18 year old marine was recovered about 24 hours later and 5 miles north of where he disappeared in a strong rip current.

The driver of a Sport Utility Vehicle missed a curve while driving along a winding dirt road in the hills near San Marcos and plunged 300 feet down the hillside. All four occupants were injured and the vehicle destroyed. Dense fog was present and hampered search and rescue efforts for several hours later.

Smoke and the proximity of flames prompting the evacuation of students at a college in Yucaipa. The winds also blew dust and debris across freeways between Fontana and Rialto during the morning commute, stopping traffic at times.

A three acre brush fire threatened to burn some outbuildings and livestock before it was brought under control.
A 182 acre brush fire burned near the central part of the base, but no structure were threatened or personnel injured.
Dense fog in Ramona contributed to a traffic accident. A 12 year old boy was struck by a car while crossing a street on the way to school.

Dense fog with visibility less than 50 feet was present between March AFB and Romoland. A three mile stretch of southbound Interstate 215 in Perris was closed for three hours to unsnarl 13 separate pileups involving at least 39 vehicles. Eight people were transported to hospitals with moderate injuries.
Sixty acres were burnt by a brush fire near Lake Elsinore. No injuries or evacuations were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm, based on radar reports of 40 VIL and 63 DBz, prompted unverified severe thunderstorm warnings for higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada in remote areas of NE Madera and NE Mariposa Counties during the early morning hours of the 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-tropical moisture streamed over portions of Interior Central California during the 3rd.  This prompted a Flash Flood Watch due to expected locally heavy rain in the Kern County Deserts and Mountains.  Only small amounts of rain fell on the San Joaquin Valley floor but this did cause concern from agriculture during the crop harvest season.  No damage was reported.

Sub-tropical moisture streamed over portions of Interior Central California during the 3rd.  This prompted a Flash Flood Watch due to expected locally heavy rain in the Kern County Deserts and Mountains.  Only small amounts of rain fell on the San Joaquin Valley floor but this did cause concern from agriculture during the crop harvest season.  No damage was reported.

A wild fire consumed three acres in steep terrain. It took eight fire engines, two air tankers, one helicopter, and 120 firefighters to control the blaze and keep it from reaching nearby structures and the National Forest.
Hail, one half inch diameter, and brief rain reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful thunderstorm produced strong winds that blew mobile homes off their foundations and knocked down 22 power poles that left about 3000 people without power. About 12 mobile homes were severely damaged and two cars were crushed by a toppled mobile home. Several roads around town were closed due to debris in the roadway.

The cost to contain the Burnt Fire is unknown.  This lightning caused fire started on July 30th but no spot forecasts were requested to fight the fire until August 11th.  The fire burned 1879 acres from July 30th through September 28th.  The fire was managed for resource benefit.  No fatalities, injuries, or structures were lost.

The Creek Fire was an arson fire that burned 11,500 acres. There were no fatalities but 11 injuries and 8 structures were lost (4 residences and 4 out-buildings).  At one point at least 400 people were forced to evacuate due to the fire and it threatened at least 3000 structures.
The Musick Fire was ignited by an unknown cause and burned 193 acres. The cost to contain was $800K but no fatalities, injuries, or structures were lost.
The North Fork Fire was ignited by a rail-splitter accidentally and burned 4132 acres southeast of Bass Lake. There were two primary structures lost but no fatalities or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A car on Highway 299 sparked the blaze near Oregon Mountain summit that was fanned by strong winds.  Final fire size was about 1680 acres.  Two-thirds of Weaverville's 3,000 residents were evacuated for a time.  Thirteen homes, thirteen outbuildings, and 29 vehicles were destroyed.
A brush fire consumed 150 acres and threatened 12 homes before being brought under control by more than 100 firefighters.
A brush fire consumed one acre along a fence line and briefly threatened homes. No structures were burned.
A brush fire just north of Vail Lake Dam consumed 20 acres.
A brush fire burned 12 acres.
One man was overcome by heat as he hiked along Lyken Trail. Heat Indices were over 115 degrees.
A brush fire consumed 2 acres and threatened several homes on Dye Road near Highway 67.

Two bolts of lightning struck a furniture store in Montclair, causing a fire that burnt it to the ground. In Ranch Cucamonga, lightning also struck a cement truck. A 33 year old man working next to the truck was thrown five feet backward, and complained of numbness in his arms. The truck driver sitting in the cab, was unharmed.
Lightning strikes in Ontario ignited a palm tree and a utility pole, and also knocked down power lines in Fontana.
Heavy downpours during a 5 minute period caused flooding of roads.
Heavy downpours from slow moving thunderstorms caused flooding along Highway 62 and Highway 247.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Water 2 to 3 feet deep covered roads in Lucerne Valley, damaging several vehicles. In Apple Valley, five people were rescued from an overturned vehicle and another two people from a flooded vehicle about a quarter mile away.
Spotters observed three inches of rain in 90 minutes. Highway 2 had mud and rock slides, and Lone Pine Canyon Road at Sheep Creek was flooded by 18 inches of water.
Flash flooding swept one car off the road in Phelan and destroyed several other roads across the Apple and Yucca valleys.

Heavy rain from slow moving thunderstorms caused flooding along Highway 74 at Vista Point, along Palm Canyon Wash between Palm Springs and Catherdal City, and washed out Highway 86 in Indio between Avenue 66 and Avenue 80. Several motorists were stranded but no injuries occurred. The American Canal overflowed in Thermal flooding a nearby elementary school and roads.

A downdraft from a high based thunderstorm ripped off large sections of tile roofing on a house, toppled a radio tower and satellite dish, and knocked over several pine trees. Observers heard a moaning sound and a wall of sand and dust moving towards them just before the gust hit.
A brush fire consumed 5 acres along Central and Monte vista avenues in the Chino Hills area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong monsoonal thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding across the mountains of Ventura county. Flash flooding was reported along Lockwood Valley Road about 2 miles west of Mutau Flat Road. Some shoulder damage occurred.

Strong monsoonal thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding across the mountains of Ventura county. Lockwood Valley Road, between Highway 33 and Chico Larson Road, was closed due to flash flooding.

Another fast moving but significant frontal system brought13"-40" of new snow to elevations above 7000 feet MSL in the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tulare County Mountains.  Larger amounts that fell in less than 12 hours included; 40" at Casa Vieja Meadow at 8400', 31" at Chagoopa Plateau at 10300', and 28" at Pascoes at 9150' in Tulare County; and, 31" at Upper Burnt Corral at 9700', and 29" at West Woodchuck Meadow at 9100' in the Southern Sierra Nevada.

The observation above verified a Heavy Snow Warning, but apparently a good bit of it fell "between" warnings.  A Winter Storm Warning was issued for zone CAZ082 above 4000 feet at 1258 PST on 12/17, lowered to 3500 feet at 0422 PST on 12/18, and expired at 1900 PST on 12/18.  The next Winter Storm Warning was issued at 0620 PST on 12/19.  This report came in during the early hours of that warning, indicating that the snow fell in the previous 24 hours.  A snow measuring device at Sand Flat at 6750 feet indicated that the bulk of the snow fell between 0600 PST and 1800 PST on 12/18, so it appears that this warning was cancelled prematurely.  Other than the above report, however, all reported amounts did not meet Heavy Snow Warning criteria.

A 24 year old man became disoriented from dehydration while hiking along the Pacific Crest Trail between Highway 74 and Idyllwild. Daytime temperatures were in the low to mid 90s at the 4500 foot elevation and the relative humidity was less than 15 percent. A Search and Rescue effort was launched after he failed to return that evening. He was found the following morning around 6 a.m.

An outflow boundary from thunderstorms associated with Tropical Depression Juliette over the Northern Gulf of California moved northwest across Eastern Riverside County. Wind gusts were measured at 39 mph at the Palm Springs International Airport. Trees and power lines were knocked down across the Coachella Valley.  Visibility was reduced to zero in blowing dust.

Part of the remnants of Hurricane Flossie, moved across Southwest California over a three day period.On 09/01/01, thunderstorms formed over the mountains and deserts of San Diego and Riverside Counties. Many reported small hail ranging in diameter from a quarter inch to a half inch. On 9/2/01, thunderstorms formed over all the mountains. Rainfall of over 2 inches in one hour was observed. Flooding and mudslides across major roadways occurred between Barton Flats and Forest Falls, from Oak Glen south to Cherry Valley  and into the Banning Pass near Beaumont, and around Lake Cuyamaca. Frequent lightning was recorded, with lightning detection sensors indicating about 60 strikes per hour. Numerous small fires were started in the National Forests. A group of four hikers climbing Stonewall Peak in the San Diego County Mountains encountered lightning and heavy rain. Two of the hikers stopped short of the peak and took shelter. Two others continued on to the lookout area which is surrounded by a metal railing. The 53 year old man was struck in the head by lightning and died. The other, a 13 year old boy, was knocked unconscious but survived. Small hail was observed by many. A downdraft from a thunderstorm knocked a tree onto a house in Beaumont. On 9/3/01, afternoon thunderstorms formed over the San Bernardino Mountains and the Apple Valley. Two boys in an open field in Apple Valley were hit by lightning. The 13 year old was eating from a cup of noodles when lightning hit him in the head and exited his groin area. He was pronounced dead some time later after CPR efforts were unsuccessful. The 9 year old boy standing next to him was holding a metal tool  at the time. He "felt the lightning pick (him) up", and then awoke lying on the ground unable to feel his legs. He suffered burns to his right waist, left calf, left foot, and dehydration. Heavy downpours from the same thunderstorm caused street flooding as it moved further east.
Spotter reports of wind gusting to 73 and 68 MPH respectively in the town of Walker.

Several brush fires were fueled by daytime temperatures over 100 degrees and dry east winds. The Duncan Canyon fire, near the mouth of Lytle Creek, burned over 200 acres and one home. The Devils Canyon Fire, north of California State University - San Bernardino, burned 150 acres. The fire southwest of Idyllwild was caused by a car rolling down an embankment, 60 acres were burned. The fire in South San Diego County scorched hillsides along Interstate 805 and made a run towards auto-dismantling yards in the Otay Mesa area before being controlled.

A 24 foot cruiser was capsized by large waves at the Oceanside Harbor entrance. The three occupants were thrown into the water and suffered minor injuries. The boat did not hit the jetty, but the breaking waves caused major damage to the transom, large cracks in the hull, and partially tore the cuddy cabin from the hull.

Two women kayakers, ages 39 and 36, were separated from their kayaks by large waves. They were able to swim to the 1/2 mile channel buoy outside of Oceanside Harbor where they were rescued by Coast Guard reservists. Both were suffering from moderate to severe hypothermia.

Cold frontal passage brought the first significant snows to the upper elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tulare County Mountains during the 30th.  A Winter Storm Watch was posted early on the 28th and resulting snowfall on the 30th provided:  13 inches of snow at Upper Burnt Corral at 9775 feet MSL and Bishop Pass at 11,200' Ft in the South Sierra Nevada; and, 10" at Upper Tyndall Creek at 10,800 Ft in the Tulare County Mountains.  About 4500 customers went without power in the South San Joaquin Valley with this first seasonal rain.  Rainfall totals in the Central and South Valley ranged from 2-4 tenths of an inch on Tuesday the 30th.

Cold frontal passage brought the first significant snows to the upper elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tulare County Mountains during the 30th.  A Winter Storm Watch was posted early on the 28th and resulting snowfall on the 30th provided:  13 inches of snow at Upper Burnt Corral at 9775 feet MSL and Bishop Pass at 11,200' Ft in the South Sierra Nevada; and, 10" at Upper Tyndall Creek at 10,800 Ft in the Tulare County Mountains. Part of the remnants of Hurricane Flossie, moved across Southwest California over a three day period.On 09/01/01, thunderstorms formed over the mountains and deserts of San Diego and Riverside Counties. Many reported small hail ranging in diameter from a quarter inch to a half inch. On 9/2/01, thunderstorms formed over all the mountains. Rainfall of over 2 inches in one hour was observed. Flooding and mudslides across major roadways occurred between Barton Flats and Forest Falls, from Oak Glen south to Cherry Valley  and into the Banning Pass near Beaumont, and around Lake Cuyamaca. Frequent lightning was recorded, with lightning detection sensors indicating about 60 strikes per hour. Numerous small fires were started in the National Forests. A group of four hikers climbing Stonewall Peak in the San Diego County Mountains encountered lightning and heavy rain. Two of the hikers stopped short of the peak and took shelter. Two others continued on to the lookout area which is surrounded by a metal railing. The 53 year old man was struck in the head by lightning and died. The other, a 13 year old boy, was knocked unconscious but survived. Small hail was observed by many. A downdraft from a thunderstorm knocked a tree onto a house in Beaumont. On 9/3/01, afternoon thunderstorms formed over the San Bernardino Mountains and the Apple Valley. Two boys in an open field in Apple Valley were hit by lightning. The 13 year old was eating from a cup of noodles when lightning hit him in the head and exited his groin area. He was pronounced dead some time later after CPR efforts were unsuccessful. The 9 year old boy standing next to him was holding a metal tool  at the time. He "felt the lightning pick (him) up", and then awoke lying on the ground unable to feel his legs. He suffered burns to his right waist, left calf, left foot, and dehydration. Heavy downpours from the same thunderstorm caused street flooding as it moved further east.

Several brush fires were fueled by daytime temperatures over 100 degrees and dry east winds. The Duncan Canyon fire, near the mouth of Lytle Creek, burned over 200 acres and one home. The Devils Canyon Fire, north of California State University - San Bernardino, burned 150 acres. The fire southwest of Idyllwild was caused by a car rolling down an embankment, 60 acres were burned. The fire in South San Diego County scorched hillsides along Interstate 805 and made a run towards auto-dismantling yards in the Otay Mesa area before being controlled.

The season's first Santa Ana winds fanned several brush fires, and started several others by banging power lines together. Smoke and the proximity of flames prompting the evacuation of students at a college in Yucaipa. The winds also blew dust and debris across freeways between Fontana and Rialto during the morning commute, stopping traffic at times.
Dense fog along the coast led to a 36 foot sailboat running aground on a reef near Cardiff. Dense fog was also observed in Fountain Valley.
Widespread dense fog over the San Diego County coastal area and lower inland valleys.
Dense ground fog.

Ocean Beach Pier was slightly damaged by waves breaking over the top of it. The Crystal Pier in Pacific Beach was also closed due to high waves.

A brush fire, started by a house electrical fire, was pushed by Santa Ana winds that reached 50 mph. The blaze quickly grew out of control, consuming several hundred acres within in a few hours. A total of 1200 acres were burned before it was fully contained three days later. Two houses, six mobile homes, several outbuildings, and motor vehicles were destroyed. Most of the town's residents were evacuated. Only one injury occurred to a resident from smoke inhalation. Over 300 firefighters fought to protect property and quench the fire, the estimated cost of fighting the fire was $616,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Winter Storm Warning was issued for CAZ080/082 above 3000 feet at 2225 PST on 11/30/01 and raised to 3500 feet at 0430 PST on 12/01/01.  At 1726 PST on 12/01, it was downgraded to a Heavy Snow Warning.  At 0400 on 12/02/01, the warning was cancelled for CAZ080 and CAZ082 was upgraded to a Winter Storm Warning.  It did snow and the wind did blow, but the reports from that zone only verified wind and snow advisories.  The reports above actually came from zones CAZ081 and CAZ084, but conditions that met warning criteria were likely not widespread as the above reports were the only ones received.  The warning was cancelled at 0430 PST on 12/03/01.
